text.title: ab-testing-basics Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for ab-testing-basics. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Define every experiment as a structured object with id, hypothesis, variants, metrics, target_sample_size, and owner before writing any assignment code.
rule.rationale: Locking the spec before code prevents post-hoc changes to allocation or primary metric, which invalidate statistical guarantees.

rule.statement: Validate before launch: variant allocations must sum to 1.0, at least 2 variants required, exactly 1 primary metric.
rule.rationale: Multiple primary metrics enable HARKing (Hypothesizing After Results are Known); no primary metric means there is no pre-registered success criterion.

rule.statement: Compute required sample size from baseline rate, MDE (in absolute pp), alpha, and power before setting target_sample_size — never use a round number as the stopping rule.
rule.rationale: Stopping early on "enough data" without power calculation inflates false positives; round-number stopping rules are not tied to detectability.

rule.statement: Bucket users using hashlib.md5(f"{experiment_id}:{user_id}".encode()).hexdigest() and bucket = (hash_value % 10000) / 10000.0 — never use random.random() or Python's built-in hash().
rule.rationale: Python's hash() is process-salted (changes per restart). random.random() produces different variants for the same user across requests. Both break session continuity and make analysis impossible.

rule.statement: Gate the variant override path behind is_staff or an env check — never expose it as an unauthenticated query parameter in production.
rule.rationale: An unauthenticated override lets any user force themselves into a variant, biasing the sample and creating a security bypass.

rule.statement: Emit an exposure event on the first variant evaluation in a session; record separately from conversion — analyses run on exposures, not raw assignments.
rule.rationale: Assignment is "what would they get"; exposure is "did they actually see it". Diluting analysis with unexposed users underestimates lift.
